title_en: Is Hurry the Great Enemy of Spiritual Life?
title_zh: 匆忙真是属灵生活的大敌吗？
rubric_en: Pastor John Mark Comer has won a massive audience by encouraging his followers to free themselves from the gnawing sense that there is always more to do.
rubric_zh: 约翰·马克·科默（John Mark Comer）赢得了庞大的受众，因为他鼓励追随者摆脱那种总觉得还有更多事情要做的折磨人感受。

paragraphs.en: John Mark Comer says his practices should be judged not by how happy his followers are, but by how close they are to God. (Thalía Gochez for The Atlantic)
paragraphs.zh: 约翰·马克·科默说，他的事奉和方法不应以信徒是否快乐来衡量，而应看他们与上帝的距离有多近。（照片：Thalía Gochez，《大西洋月刊》）

paragraphs.en: John Mark Comer can be a hard man to find. He’s one of the most famous pastors in America right now, an author whose books have together sold more than 1 million copies, but he’s not the most reachable guy. He has a professional website but no contact page. He rarely travels. And as I reported this story, I began to learn his habits: Sending him a text early in the day was a wash, for instance, because he doesn’t check his phone until after morning prayer time. Once, when I reached out by email, I got an out-of-office response that he had set before Christmas explaining that he was observing “rhythms of rest” and asking that I try him again after his return in mid-January. Incoming messages sent in the meantime would be deleted.
paragraphs.zh: 要找到约翰·马克·科默并不容易。如今他是美国最著名的牧师之一，也是一本合计销量超过100万册的畅销作家，但他并不算容易接触。他有一个专业网站，却没有联系页面；他很少出差。就在我采访这篇故事时，我开始熟悉他的作息：比如说清晨发一条短信往往于事无补，因为他在早间祷告时间后才看手机。有一次我发了电子邮件，却收到一封他在圣诞节前设置的自动回复，说明他在实践“休息节律”，并请我在1月中旬他回来后再联系。期间收到的来信会被删除。

paragraphs.en: I had first seen Comer in October, at a service for Church of the City New York, held inside a historic chapel in Lower Manhattan. Lo-fi beats played over the speakers as hundreds of people, mostly in their 20s and 30s, milled around and looked for seats in the crammed pews. When Comer took the stage, dressed in a matching ochre shirt-jacket and pants, a silver stud in his left ear, the crowd cheered and whooped.
paragraphs.zh: 我第一次见到科默是在10月，在纽约下城区一座历史悠久的小礼拜堂里举行的“纽约都会教会”聚会上。音响里播放着低保真节拍，数百名以20多岁和30多岁为主的听众在拥挤的长凳间走动、找座位。当科默登上讲台，身穿同色系赭黄衬衫外套和长裤，左耳上戴着一枚银色耳钉时，人群欢呼并大声尖叫。

paragraphs.en: He pulled up a slide. It was not the usual Bible story or psalm, but an excerpt from Anne Helen Petersen’s 2019 BuzzFeed essay “How Millennials Became the Burnout Generation.” Burnout is “not a temporary affliction,” it read. “It’s the millennial condition.” The Gen Z one, too, Comer added. “It’s like we just churn out tired, exhausted souls like a widget factory,” he said. “I don’t know if you feel this at all yet in your body or in your bones. If you don’t, it’s because you’re still young and you haven’t been in the city very long. But you will. Trust me, you will.”
paragraphs.zh: 他放出了一张幻灯片。这不是平常的圣经故事或诗篇，而是安妮·海伦·彼得森（Anne Helen Petersen）2019年《BuzzFeed》文章《千禧一代如何成为“倦怠一代”》中的一段摘录。内容写道：“倦怠并非一时的困扰。”“它是千禧一代的普遍状况。”科默补充道，“Z世代也是如此。”他说：“这就像是我们在流水线工厂一样，不断产出疲惫不堪的灵魂。” “我不知道你是否已经在身体或骨子里感受到这一点。如果你还没感觉到，那是因为你还年轻，或者在城市里待的时间还不长。但你会的。相信我，你会的。”

paragraphs.en: Then he clicked over to a passage from the Gospel of Matthew:
paragraphs.zh: 接着他翻到《马太福音》中的一段经文。

paragraphs.en: “Most of us, as modern Americans,” Comer said, with a hand over his heart, “we read that line and there’s just this, like, deep, soul-level, Yes, I ache for that.” The guy in front of me took a picture of the slide with his phone. I noticed that his screen was set to gray scale. So was the screen of the person sitting next to me.
paragraphs.zh: “我们大多数像现代美国人一样，”科默说着，一只手放在胸前，“读到这句话时，都会有一种很深、很心灵层面的感觉。是啊，这让我很心动。” 我前面那位男士拿起手机给幻灯片拍照。我注意到他的屏幕是灰度模式。我旁边那位的屏幕也是灰度的。

paragraphs.en: Read: The tension that defines modern life
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：定义现代生活的紧张感

paragraphs.en: Signs of Comer’s influence had been popping up in my life all year. One friend had started observing a 24-hour, phone-free Sabbath. My roommates began fasting several times a month. Then, in quick succession, three different people recommended that I read The Ruthless Elimination of Hurry, Comer’s 2019 best seller.
paragraphs.zh: 我整整一年里，在生活中不断看到科默影响力的迹象。一个朋友开始坚持24小时无手机的安息日；我的室友也开始每月禁食几次。随后，又有三位不同的人相继建议我阅读科默2019年的畅销书《无情清除匆忙》。

paragraphs.en: In that book, Comer advances the theory that the great enemy of spiritual life is hurry. By this he means not simply busyness: Hurry is a gnawing sense that there is always more to do; a life spent hurtling oneself through each day; a schedule that makes little room for God. Technology has only exacerbated the problem. Comer calls the modern world “a virtual conspiracy against the interior life,” and urges readers to reclaim their focus from the algorithm and shift it toward God.
paragraphs.zh: 在那本书里，科默提出一个理论：属灵生活最大的敌人是匆忙。就这一点而言，他指的不仅仅是“忙碌”本身；匆忙是一种不断被啃噬的感觉——总觉得每天还得做更多事情；一种把自己推着在每一天中飞驰前进的生活方式；一张几乎不给上帝留出空间的日程表。科技只会让这个问题更严重。科默把现代世界称为“对内在生命的“虚拟阴谋”，并敦促读者把注意力从算法手里夺回来，转向上帝。

paragraphs.en: The Ruthless Elimination of Hurry, he told me, is “a book about discipleship to Jesus masquerading as a self-help book.” Many of its suggestions are similar to what you might find in articles about digital detoxes. To break a cellphone addiction, he offers detailed advice on how to “turn your smartphone into a dumbphone”: delete social media and web browsers, turn off notifications, and set your screen to gray scale, to curb the appeal of the remaining candy-colored apps. His prose, too, is rendered in a pithy, how-to style that one of his critics has dubbed “The Ruthless Elimination of Paragraphs.”
paragraphs.zh: 科默告诉我，《无情淘汰匆忙》是“一本伪装成自助书的门徒训练书”。它中的许多建议与您在数字排毒相关文章里看到的内容相似。为了戒掉对手机的上瘾，他提供了很具体的方法，比如把智能手机“变成小白机”：删除社交媒体和网页浏览器、关闭推送通知、把屏幕调成灰度，以削弱剩下那些五颜六色应用的吸引力。他的文风同样是那种简洁直接、操作性很强的“如何做”风格，甚至被一位批评者称为“《无情淘汰匆忙》中的《无情淘汰段落》”。

paragraphs.en: Because of this approach, Comer can seem more like a wellness personality, such as Andrew Huberman, than a pastor. Like Huberman, Comer offers a concrete regimen that’s attractive to people who feel unmoored in contemporary society. Comer’s skeptics, when remarking on his rapid ascent, point to these similarities and wonder if what he’s offering is simply baptized wellness, a pop spirituality tailored to the tastes and frustrations of affluent young people. But sitting among his followers, I wondered: Could Comer’s practices actually bring them closer to God?
paragraphs.zh: 正因为这种表达方式，科默有时看起来更像一个健康管理类公众人物，比如安德鲁·休伯曼，而不是牧师。像休伯曼一样，科默也提供一套具体的实践方案，这对当代社会里感到失去支点的人具有很大吸引力。科默的怀疑者在谈到他快速崛起时，会提到这些相似之处，并猜测他所提供的是否只是一种“受洗的健康文化”，一种迎合富裕年轻人口味和挫败感的流行灵性产品。但我在他追随者中间时在想：科默的这些做法，真的能让他们离上帝更近吗？

paragraphs.en: I met Comer the next day at a coffee shop in the East Village. Our cashier, who looked about 24, recognized Comer and was visibly starstruck. “Your books are so amazing,” he said. “I pass them around to all my friends.” Our lattes, he insisted, were on the house. Comer told me that the same thing had happened yesterday in SoHo, then he shrugged. “Coffee shops are like bars for Christians.”
paragraphs.zh: 我第二天在东村的一家咖啡馆见到了科默。收银员看起来大约24岁，认出他后明显显得像个粉丝。“你的书太棒了，”他说，“我都会拿去给朋友看。” 他坚持说，我们的拿铁都免单。科默告诉我，昨天在苏荷也发生过同样的事，然后他耸了耸肩：“对基督徒来说，咖啡店就像是酒吧。”

paragraphs.en: Comer is Protestant, nondenominational, and roughly in the evangelical sphere, but his work is mostly about how technology—what he calls “the machine”—is spiritually deforming people. “Any version of discipleship to Jesus that doesn’t seriously take into account that,” he said, pointing at my phone, “is going to be wildly deficient.” Christian spirituality has always adapted to its time, Comer said. In trying to adapt the faith for the 21st century, he looks to the life of Jesus, who took a Sabbath, fasted, and spent regular time in silence and solitude. To Comer, these weren’t the rhythms of Jesus’s life just because he happened to live in Galilee in 30 C.E. They are spiritual practices that Christians in any era ought to emulate.
paragraphs.zh: 科默是新教、非宗派的，且大致属于福音派圈层，但他的作品主要聚焦于技术——他称之为“机器”——如何在属灵层面塑造并扭曲人。 “任何一种关于耶稣门徒训练的说法，如果没有认真把这一点放在核心位置，”他边指着我的手机边说， “都将严重失准。” 科默说，基督教灵修历来都会与时代相适应。在努力让信仰适应21世纪的过程中，他回到耶稣的生活样式：守安息日、禁食，并且规律地独处与守静默。对科默来说，这些并不是耶稣之所以身在加利利、生活在公元30年才有的节律；而是任何时代的基督徒都应当效法的属灵操练。

paragraphs.en: Read: How to break a phone addiction
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：如何戒掉手机成瘾

paragraphs.en: Comer’s most recent book, 2024’s Practicing the Way, is a sort of how-to guide for Christlike living. Inspired in part by the monastic Order of Saint Benedict, Comer encourages readers to incorporate nine of Jesus’s habits into their lives: scripture reading, service, keeping the Sabbath, solitude, prayer, fasting, community, witness, and generosity. He calls his work “spiritual archaeology”—reintroducing modern believers to ancient Christian practices. “Everything we need, for the most part, is there in church history,” he said. “We’ve just lost a lot of it.”
paragraphs.zh: 科默最新的著作是2024年的《实践之道》，有点像一本“如何做”手册，指导人们如何过基督化的生活。该书在一定程度上受圣本笃会修道传统启发，科默鼓励读者把九项耶稣的习惯纳入日常生活：读经、服事、守安息日、独处、祈祷、禁食、群体生活、作见证与慷慨。他把自己的工作称为“属灵考古学”——即把现代信徒重新带回到古代基督教的灵修传统。 “就大多数方面而言，我们所需的一切都在教会历史中都能找到。”他表示， “只是我们失去得太多了。”

paragraphs.en: Comer is hardly the first such archaeologist. Each generation of evangelical Christianity has three main celebrities, Russell Moore, the editor at large of Christianity Today, told me: the politics guy, the church-growth guy, and the personal-spirituality guy. In the 1980s, these roles were played, respectively, by Pat Robertson, Rick Warren, and Dallas Willard. Right now, Comer is the personal-spirituality guy (yes, it’s always a guy). Willard encouraged evangelicals to adopt virtually the same practices, such as fasting and taking a Sabbath, in 1988’s The Spirit of the Disciplines, and a subset of evangelicals has practiced them ever since. But Comer is making his case at a very different moment.
paragraphs.zh: 科默并不是这类“考古学家”中的第一人。美国《今日基督教》杂志执行编辑拉塞尔·摩尔告诉我，福音派基督教每一代都有三位主要人物：政治人物、教会增长人物，以及个人灵命人物。20世纪80年代，这三种角色分别由帕特·罗伯森、里克·沃伦和达拉斯·威拉德担任。如今，科默就是那个“个人灵命人物”（是的，通常总是“他”）。威拉德在1988年《灵修的精神》中也鼓励福音派采取几乎相同的操练，比如禁食和守安息日，并且从那时起便有一部分福音派持续实践这些。可科默是在一个非常不同的历史时刻发出他的呼吁。

paragraphs.en: Russell Moore: The American evangelical Church is in crisis. There’s only one way out.
paragraphs.zh: 拉塞尔·摩尔：美国的福音派教会正陷入危机。它只有一条出路。

paragraphs.en: “A lot of American evangelical leadership right now is algorithmic,” Moore said, meaning that many pastors ratchet up their sermon rhetoric to find an audience on social media—usually by decrying homosexuality and abortion. Comer has written that God’s vision of marriage is between a man and a woman, and he’s argued against the idea of abortion as “reproductive justice.” But he doesn’t really preach about those issues, so the traditional Christian political camps aren’t sure what to make of him. He’s too conservative for the progressive Christians, and the conservative ones assume that he’s a tote-bag-carrying NPR liberal.
paragraphs.zh: “现在许多美国福音派领袖都在‘算法化’运作，”摩尔说，这意味着许多牧师会不断提升布道的煽动性言辞，以在社交媒体上吸引听众——通常是通过抨击同性恋和堕胎。科默写过文，认为上帝对婚姻的设想是“一男一女”，并反对把堕胎包装成“生育正义”这一说法。但他并不真正宣传这些议题，因此传统的基督教政治阵营都拿他无从下手。对进步派基督徒来说，他太保守；而保守派则以为他是个拎着帆布袋、受NPR影响的自由派。

paragraphs.en: Comer doesn’t avoid the algorithm entirely. He has more than a quarter million followers on Instagram, where he mostly posts clips about the nine practices and shares quotes from Christian writers in minimalist fonts on earth-toned slides. He likens such social-media outreach to a street preacher at an Old West saloon: You say your piece about Jesus, hope you change some minds, and get out as quickly as you can.
paragraphs.zh: 科默并没有完全回避“算法”。他在Instagram上有超过二十五万名关注者，主要发布关于九个灵修操练的短视频，并用极简字体在大地色幻灯片上分享基督教作者的语录。他把这种社交媒体传播比作旧西部酒吧前的街头布道者：你站出来讲一句关于耶稣的话，尽量说服几个人改变想法，然后尽快离开。

paragraphs.en: In December, I went to Comer’s house for tea. About two and a half years ago, his family moved from Portland, Oregon, to Topanga Canyon, a mountain community outside Los Angeles known as a hub of West Coast hippiedom—think Deadheads, crystals, and astral-projection workshops. The road to Comer’s home is shaded by scrub oak and barely wide enough to accommodate a single car. We sat in the living room beside the Christmas tree, where presents lay wrapped in butcher paper. Comer was on cooking duty that night, and his wife unloaded the groceries. Their teenage son and daughter milled around the living room as Comer and I spoke. He apologized for the commotion.
paragraphs.zh: 12月，我去科默家喝茶。大约两年半前，他的家人从俄勒冈州波特兰搬到了洛杉矶外的东安加峡谷（Topanga Canyon），那里是西海岸嬉皮文化的中心地之一——可以联想到“死忠乐迷”“水晶”以及“星体投影”工作坊。这条通往他家的路两旁长满了低矮橡树，几乎只够一辆车并行。我们在客厅里的圣诞树旁坐下，礼物裹在蜡纸里摆着。那晚轮到科默负责做饭，他妻子正在卸下从商店带回来的杂货。儿子和女儿两个十几岁的孩子在客厅里来回走动着，我和科默交谈时，他们就在我们周围走来走去。他向我道歉，抱歉打扰。

paragraphs.en: Comer grew up in the ’80s in Silicon Valley; his parents were “first-generation Christians,” as he put it. His father, Phil, was a rock musician in the ’60s and ’70s who encountered God for the first time during one of Billy Graham’s crusades, eventually becoming the worship pastor at Los Gatos Christian Church, one of the Bay Area’s earliest evangelical megachurches. Comer took after his dad, joining the ministry and then co-founding a church in the suburbs of Portland with his parents in 2003, when he was 23 years old. Comer was the cool preacher, a West Coast urbanite just like his congregants; he understood why people might be cynical about religion. (When we met, I apologized for saying “damn” in front of a pastor. He reminded me that I was with a pastor from California.)
paragraphs.zh: 科默在20世纪80年代成长于硅谷；他把父母称作“第一代基督徒”。他的父亲菲尔在20世纪60至70年代是一位摇滚乐手，后来在比利·格雷厄姆（Billy Graham）的一场次布道会上第一次遇见上帝，最终成为洛斯盖图斯基督教会的敬拜牧师，而这所教会是旧金山湾区最早的福音派大教堂之一。科默追随父亲的脚步，加入事工，并在2003年与父母一起在波特兰郊区共同创办了一所教会，那时他23岁。科默是个很“酷”的讲道者，像他的会众一样是西海岸的都市人；他能理解人们为何会对宗教持怀疑态度。（我们见面时，我向一位牧师道歉，说自己在牧师面前说了“damn”这个词，他提醒我，我当时的对话对象是位加州牧师。）

paragraphs.en: His church added about 1,000 congregants a year for seven years straight and soon outgrew its original building, coming to command multiple locations around the city. Comer became the head of what was essentially a ministry franchise, he reflected later—“the Starbucks model of ‘local’ church”—where he was trying to give thousands of people the same experience, whether they were in downtown Portland or the suburbs.
paragraphs.zh: 他的教会连续七年每年新增约1,000名会众，很快超出了原有建筑的容量，进而在城市周边扩展到多个崭新的聚会点。科默后来回想，他成了本质上一个事工连锁机构的负责人——“像星巴克模式那样的‘本地化’教会”——他试图让成千上万的人在城中心的波特兰或郊区都能拥有同样的经历。

paragraphs.en: By about 2014, Comer was preaching six services on Sundays and heading home at 10 p.m., long after his kids were asleep. He didn’t have time for himself or his family. The Bible calls Christians to be patient, to love. But Comer was becoming more hurried and less loving. He realized, as he would later write, that “you can be a success as a pastor and a failure as an apprentice of Jesus.” In Millennial terms, he was suffering from burnout, badly.
paragraphs.zh: 到了2014年前后，科默在周日要讲六场主日崇拜，晚上10点才回家，孩子们早已入睡。他几乎没有时间给自己或家人。圣经呼吁基督徒要有耐心，要去爱。可科默却变得越来越匆促，也越来越少爱。后来他在文字里写道，他意识到“你可以在牧师身份上成功，却在作耶稣门徒这条路上失败”。用千禧一代的话说，他已经严重地遭遇了职业倦怠。

paragraphs.en: Comer took a break from preaching and started reorganizing his life. He tried to emulate Christ’s daily actions, gradually incorporating them into his lifestyle both then and after he returned to pastoring, now at just one of the church’s locations, known as Bridgetown Church, in downtown Portland. He began fasting, eventually working up to two days a week, and observing the Sabbath by turning off his devices on Saturdays and spending his time resting and worshipping. He still needed to use email and social media for work, but he took these apps off his phone and checked them on his computer only once a week. And because Jesus lived simply, Comer pared down his closet to three outfits for the Oregon winter and two for the summer.
paragraphs.zh: 科默暂时停止讲道，开始重新整理自己的生活。他尝试效法基督的日常行动，逐步把这种作法融入自己的生活方式——无论是在此后仍以波特兰市中心布里奇敦教会（Bridgetown Church）这一单一堂点继续带领事工前，还是回到讲台之后。

paragraphs.en: He worked less, spent more time with his wife, built more Star Wars Lego sets with his kids. “Even better,” he’d later write about that period, he could “feel God again.” Comer was convinced that his entire church would benefit from these practices. So, over the next five years, Bridgetown adopted the disciplines as a congregation, creating the blueprint for the nine practices that Comer later would lay out in Practicing the Way.
paragraphs.zh: 他减少了工作量，花更多时间陪伴妻子，还和孩子一起搭建了更多《星球大战》的乐高模型。后来他在回顾那段时期时写道，“更妙的是”，他“又能再次感受到上帝”。科默坚信整个教会都能从这些做法中受益。于是，在接下来的五年里，布里奇敦教会全体会众把这些操练作为公共实践来采用，由此形成了他后来在《实践道路》中提出的九项操练的蓝图。

paragraphs.en: Running a huge church was hard on him; for years, he had wanted to write and to work one-on-one with people instead of preaching. Comer stepped down from Bridgetown in 2021 and now leads a nonprofit, also called Practicing the Way, which offers a free course that more than 21,000 church groups have adopted. He’s on the teaching staff at a church in Los Angeles, but mostly, Comer serves as the pastor of his own small church, which follows the Practicing the Way disciplines: The 30-person congregation fasts together, takes the Sabbath together, and, on Sundays, meets for a service in his living room. He has “built a quiet life,” his friend and successor at Bridgetown, Pastor Tyler Staton, told me. “Some might accuse him of being a touch boring.”
paragraphs.zh: 管理一个大教会让他身心俱疲；多年来，他一直希望自己能更多地写作并一对一地与人交流，而不是一直接受讲道。科默于2021年从布里奇敦辞去牧师职务，现在领导一个同名非营利机构“实践道路”，该机构提供了一门免费课程，已有超过21,000个教会小组采纳。他现在在洛杉矶的一间教会担任讲师，但他主要精力放在自己所牧养的小型教会身上，该教会遵行《实践道路》的操练：30人的会众一起禁食，一起守安息日，并在周日聚在他客厅里敬拜。布里奇敦的朋友兼继任者泰勒·斯塔顿牧师告诉我，科默“过着安静的生活”。“也有人会指责他有点无聊。”

paragraphs.en: Comer told me that his average reader is 27, with at least some college education, living in a city. I’m 27, with a college degree, living in New York. I wondered whether I could adhere to his disciplines, and if so, how they might affect my faith. So, for the past six months, I’ve tried to structure my life around Practicing the Way’s nine core habits.
paragraphs.zh: 科默告诉我，他的典型读者平均年龄是27岁，至少有一些大学教育，住在城市里。我今年27岁，拥有大学学位，住在纽约。我想知道自己是否能坚持他的操练方式，如果可以，这些习惯会怎样影响我的信仰。于是，在过去六个月里，我尝试围绕《实践道路》提出的九个核心习惯来重构我的生活。

paragraphs.en: I’d wake up early to spend an hour alone at the window next to my fire escape, reading scripture and praying; this was a major upgrade from checking my phone first thing in the morning. Once a week, I’d observe the Sabbath—put away my screens, do some form of worship, revel in the fact that I could do nothing for a day and God would keep the universe going. As part of the service practice, I volunteered at a soup kitchen once a month and started carrying food with me when I walked around the city, in case I passed people who looked hungry.
paragraphs.zh: 我每天早起，在逃生通道旁的窗边独自待一个小时，读经祷告；这比起一醒来就先看手机，是一个巨大的升级。每周我都会守一次安息日——把电子屏幕放到一边，进行某种敬拜，享受这样一种事实：我可以整整一天什么都不做，而上帝会让宇宙继续运行。作为敬拜实践的一部分，我每月在流浪者厨房做志愿服务，并在步行穿过城市时随身携带食物，以防我遇见看起来饥饿的人。

paragraphs.en: I did chafe against some of the disciplines. Navigating modern life with no phone for a day was a mess: Without Google Maps, I’d get lost; without texting, every meetup with friends felt like the high-stakes rendezvous at the end of An Affair to Remember. And although sometimes I’d have a moment or two of transcendence on my weekly fasting day, for the most part, I was just hungry.
paragraphs.zh: 我确实对其中一些操练感到不适应。尝试一整天不带手机去适应现代生活很乱：没有谷歌地图，我会迷路；没有发短信，每次和朋友见面都像《情归阿富汗》里（《Love Affair to Remember》）那种惊险的末点约会。虽然每周禁食那天我有时会有一两次超然的时刻，但大多数时候我只是觉得很饿。

paragraphs.en: I am surprised, though, by how much these practices have become central to my life—not because I think I will be smote if I don’t do them, but because it turns out I like them. (Except for fasting. That one is still a bummer.) The new constraints on my time and attention forced me to truly consider what was important or not, and to prioritize those things. I spent less time on the parts of my day that brought me little joy (my phone) and more time with friends. My life is less hurried. I’m happier.
paragraphs.zh: 不过我很惊讶这些做法竟然在我的生活中变得如此核心——不是因为我觉得不做就会遭天谴，而是因为结果证明我真的喜欢它们。（禁食除外，那项至今仍让我很失落。）对我时间和注意力的新约束，迫使我真正思考什么重要、什么不重要，并据此重新排序。我在那些给我带来很少快乐的部分上花了更少时间（比如我的手机），而和朋友在一起的时间更多了。我的生活不再那么匆忙了。我更开心了。

paragraphs.en: But my happiness is not the point, according to Comer. The purpose of a spiritual discipline is “not personal fulfillment. It’s not personal expression. It’s not emotional wellness. It’s not to de-stress,” he said. The point is to have your character transformed by your attunement to God. Then it will be easier to follow Jesus’s two greatest commandments: love God and love others. Fasting and discipline, you can get from Andrew Huberman; self-care, from Goop. But, Comer told me, “wellness culture is not talking about the Sermon on the Mount.”
paragraphs.zh: 但根据科默的说法，我的快乐并不是关键。科默说，属灵操练的目的“不是个人成就。不是个人表达。也不是情绪疗愈。更不是为了减压。”关键是让你的品格在你与上帝的调谐中被改变。那样你就更容易遵行耶稣最重要的两条诫命：爱上帝和爱人。禁食和纪律，你可以向安德鲁·胡伯曼那里学；自我关怀，你可以向 Goop 里学。但科默对我说，“健康文化并没有在谈《山中宝训》。

paragraphs.en: That sermon—in which Jesus says people must love their enemies, must turn the other cheek, and cannot serve God and money—asks a lot from believers. Dallas Willard, Comer’s forebear, argued that a person who expects to live up to Jesus’s commands on the spur of the moment, without structuring their life at least somewhat around Jesus’s, is like “a baseball player who expects to excel in the game without adequate exercise of his body.” The theory is that, to become more Christlike, you have to find more ways to literally live like Christ.
paragraphs.zh: 在那篇讲道中，耶稣说人必须爱敌人、要把另一边脸也转过去，并且不能同时事奉上帝和金钱——这些要求对信徒来说都很高。达拉斯·威拉德——科默的前辈——曾指出，如果一个人指望在没有将生活至少部分按耶稣来结构的情况下，临时想到时就能做到耶稣的命令，那么他就像“一个期待不做充分训练却还能在球场上打出好成绩的棒球运动员”。他的理论是：要想更像基督，你就必须找到更多实实在在、像基督一样生活的方式。

paragraphs.en: Comer’s critics worry that by focusing so much on Jesus’s daily regimen, he risks recasting the son of God as the original lifestyle guru. “The (real) point of the Gospels—identifying who Jesus is, putting faith in him, and worshiping him—is put in the background, while living like Jesus is put in the foreground,” Kevin DeYoung, a theologian and Presbyterian pastor, wrote in a review of Practicing the Way.
paragraphs.zh: 科默（Comer）的批评者担心，他过分强调耶稣的日常生活方式，可能把上帝之子重新塑造成最早的“生活方式导师”。《实践之道》（Practicing the Way）的作者兼评论者、神学家兼长老会牧师凯文·德杨（Kevin DeYoung）写道：“福音书真正的核心——识别谁是耶稣、信靠他、并敬拜他——被放到了背景里，而“像耶稣一样生活”却被放到了前景。”

paragraphs.en: According to DeYoung, this isn’t just a small matter of emphasis. “How effective can an approach to spiritual formation be when it almost completely misses the point of Jesus’s life and ministry?” he wrote. DeYoung told me that when the apostle Paul writes to the early Ephesian church about how to combat evil in their lives, “he doesn’t tell them, ‘Here are a set of rhythms and come up with 10 rules for your life.’ ” He tells them about the power of God.
paragraphs.zh: 德杨认为，这不仅仅是强调重点上的小问题。“当一种属灵塑造的方法几乎完全忽略了耶稣一生与事奉的核心是什么时，它还能有多有效呢？”他写道。德杨告诉我，当使徒保罗写信给早期以弗所教会，教导他们如何对抗生活中的邪恶时，“他并没有对他们说：‘这是你们应当遵行的一套节律，并给你们的人生拟定十条规则。’”他告诉他们的是上帝的能力。

paragraphs.en: DeYoung and others also criticize Comer for conforming his ministry too much to the lives of young, well-to-do urbanites—repackaging Christian monasticism for the TikTok generation. Given how inconvenient Comer’s disciplines can be, his skeptics think they’re achievable for yuppies in ways they may not be for others who have fewer resources or more demands on their time. DeYoung and his wife have a big family, and although Comer’s routine may sound nice, he told me, “we’re trying to just get through our week.”
paragraphs.zh: 德杨等人还批评科默的事工过于迎合年轻、生活富足的城市人生活方式——把基督教修道主义改装成了适合 TikTok 一代的版本。考虑到科默所推崇的灵修纪律常常并不方便，他的怀疑者认为，这些做法可能对“雅皮士”而言较为可行，却不一定适用于资源更有限、或时间负担更重的人群。德杨和他的妻子有一个大家庭，尽管科默的日常安排听起来很吸引人，但他告诉我，他们“正在努力让每周生活过得下去”。

paragraphs.en: Jonathan Haidt: End the phone-based childhood now
paragraphs.zh: 乔纳森·海特：立即结束手机化的童年

paragraphs.en: Comer counters that many churches are facing what he calls a “crisis of discipleship” because they don’t give congregants enough instruction on how to actually live as Christians. But he says that he’s not doctrinaire about the practices; he doesn’t expect everyone to do all of them, all of the time: Jesus himself rebelled against the rigidity of the Pharisees by healing people and harvesting grain on the Sabbath. The night I saw Comer preach in New York City, he stressed that the question shouldn’t be Did I fast this week? or Did I observe the Sabbath? Comer wants his followers to ask themselves instead, Am I becoming more gentle? and Am I becoming more humble?
paragraphs.zh: 科默认为许多教会正面临他所说的“门徒训练危机”，因为这些教会并未为会众提供如何真正活出基督徒生命的足够指导。不过他强调自己并不教条化这些操练；他不要求每个人在任何时间都做每一项，因为耶稣自己就曾反抗法利赛人的僵硬观念，在安息日里医治人并收割庄稼。那天晚上我在纽约市听科默讲道，他着重强调，问题不应是“我这周禁食了吗？”或“我守安息日了吗？”，他希望追随者问自己的应是“我是否更温柔了？”以及“我是否更谦卑了？”

paragraphs.en: I Googled myself yesterday, so I still have a ways to go. But I had never asked myself those sorts of questions before. As a Christian moving in mostly secular circles, I’d felt that simply believing in God was a big enough feat. My faith had never shaped the way I lived each day. I am proof that you can say you love God and offer very little of your life to him. The practices became a way to call my own bluff.
paragraphs.zh: 我昨天在谷歌上搜索了自己，所以我仍然有很长的路要走。不过我以前从未这样问过自己。作为一个主要在世俗圈子活动的基督徒，我一直觉得只要相信上帝就已经很不容易了。我的信仰从未真正塑造我每天的生活方式。我就是一个例证：你可以说你爱上帝，却几乎不把你的生命交给他。那些操练成了一种让我露出“虚张声势”的方式。

paragraphs.en: I’m a member of the precise audience Comer is writing for—those who believe in the Gospels but haven’t made much time for a spiritual life; those who no longer feel at home in an evangelical community that has itself been warped by the imperatives of social media; those who (if we’re honest) can sometimes feel embarrassed to be seen as religious in a secular world. He told me that he is speaking to people who “want to figure out how to stay true to the Christian story in a very hostile cultural environment” but feel they need a road map. Even if the temptations of contemporary America look nothing like the ones the early Christian ascetics lived in the desert to avoid, that doesn’t necessarily mean the road map itself is out-of-date. And if, in promoting that road map, Comer can sometimes seem like many secular wellness influencers, maybe it’s a sign that they, too, are responding to a collective crisis of faith, and don’t yet know it.
paragraphs.zh: 我正是科默写作时瞄准的典型群体——这些人相信福音，但并没有为属灵生活腾出太多时间；这些人不再在一个本身已被社交媒体驱动逻辑扭曲的福音派群体里感到自在；这些人（如果我们诚实）在世俗世界里有时还会因为被视为“宗教人”而感到尴尬。他告诉我，他是在向那些“想在极其敌对的文化环境中弄清楚如何忠于基督故事”的人说话，但又觉得自己需要一张路线图。即使当代美国的诱惑看起来与早期基督教隐修者为了躲避而逃向沙漠的诱惑完全不同，这也不必然意味着这张路线图已经过时。而且，如果在推广这张路线图时，科默有时看起来像许多世俗的健康生活方式博主，这或许正说明他们也在回应一种普遍的信仰危机，只是自己还没意识到。

paragraphs.en: This article appears in the May 2026 print edition with the headline “Can Turning Off Your Phone Bring You Closer to God?”
paragraphs.zh: 这篇文章将刊登在 2026 年 5 月的印刷版，标题是《关掉手机会让你更接近上帝吗？》
